text: The surgeon works laterally towards Bogros space, observing a preperitoneal plane that is free of scarring and devoid of fat, allowing for easy dissection.
The surgeon works medially toward the supravesicular space, revealing the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum converging at the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon observes a thickened hernia sac extending into the deep internal ring on the medial side. Using cautery, the surgeon scores the sac and pushes fat laterally to improve visualization. The vas deferens is not yet visible, prompting further medial dissection below Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle.
The hernia sac comes into view, appearing edematous and relatively small. The surgeon notes its symptomatic nature despite its size.

The surgeon uses an oblique angle to push down the spermatic vessels, revealing the right iliac vein. The dissection is performed close to the tissue to prevent mesh buckling, emphasizing careful manipulation around the corner of the dissection area.

The iliac vein is visible on the right side of the surgical field. The surgeon assesses the tailored pocket for mesh placement, ensuring high lateral fixation to prevent mesh migration. The surgeon checks the other side of the mesh for any vascular structures.
The surgeon performs a straightforward flap closure, ensuring the edges of the tissue are aligned and secured.
